speaker: interviewer
text: We're with Joseph Lipschutz, and we're on tape six. How old were you when you were with Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born 1918 and this was 1944. So of course I was 26 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you arrived to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1949, so I was 31 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got to America, you came to New York, and then you were sent to what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spent approximately two weeks, I think, in New York, supported by the Jewish Family Service, and then sent to Philadelphia with my wife and baby. Because our visa was, I think, guaranteed by the Family Service of-- Jewish Family Service of Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Jewish Family Service of Philadelphia, when we arrived there, has prepared an apartment for us in South Philadelphia and has made arrangements for me to have a job as a furrier, where I worked for about a year, maybe a little longer, at the compensation of $1.00 per hour, which was not enough to make a living for our family. But as I said before, fortunately, I brought some money from Germany, so we lived quite decently.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you eventually get another-- a different job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, eventually I decided that I wished to become an engineer. And I would use the fur business as a vehicle to arrive there. So I got a job as a furrier in couple of different places. And started school, evening college at Drexel Institute of Technology, today known as Drexel University, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taking electrical engineering. I eventually switched jobs. Got a job as a electrical technician, and I continued going to school. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] start in the company, as I said, as a technician. And this was 1953. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked there in this company till 1980, when I retired at age 62 as a vice president of operations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of your friends in Philadelphia survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were. All of them were at the beginning, I didn't know any others. But eventually, through the places of work I met other people and we associated with others. But to be really friends with, probably it-- it would be that they were all survivors. Because they-- we understood each other. Very difficult to explain something to a person who it is impossible for that person to understand. We-- we recognize that, we don't blame people for not understanding. There's no way to understand it. If you want to know what hell is like, you have to go to hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had another child. Another daughter was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1959, we had another baby. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another girl. And that was all for us, unfortunately. A few years later, my wife got sick and passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you open with your children about your experiences as they were growing up? Did-- was it something you talked about with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was, always. They never showed any interest for it. As a matter of fact, neither did the cousin who had gone through that hell himself. Whenever I meet with him, he just doesn't want to talk about it. And he never did. I have done several things to awake my children to the fact that they are children of-- of a survivor. Of survivors, actually. My wife as well. I have done some tapes for them. I have done some tapes in which-- video which I narrate my past, like I am doing it right now. Maybe not with all that detail. But to best of my knowledge, there is not much interest in it. This is unfortunate for me. My friends, more fortunate, have children are more interested in their past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that some decisions in your life were influenced by your experience? Were you-- did you-- were you a different kind a father because of what you've been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what father would I have been if that wouldn't be through. I don't know, and I can't answer that. I can't answer that at all. I believe I was a good father. Before I got the job as electrical technician, as a matter-- [INAUDIBLE] technician, and as a matter of fact even for a while I was, I worked two or three jobs. I worked-- I got-- I had a regular job, I had an evening job, and I had a weekend job. Because things have to be gone, but I didn't want to spend all the few dollars that I made by just adding every week to what-- to our upkeep. It would have been a tragedy if I would not have made adequate progress when the money's gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share with your children stories about their-- their grandparents, and your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't, because I didn't know them myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean stories about growing up. Have they heard stories about your life as a child and the relatives that are no longer living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did-- we didn't sit down and have a discussion on the subject, but yes, of course. These things were mentioned at the one occasion or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experience changed your belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: You create a very tough one, now. I have to go back to the camp in Plaszow. In camp in Plaszow where I mentioned to you, and also in ghetto to a large degree, to some degree. At the camp in Plaszow, which I mentioned to you had a pit in which they were shooting people, and they were falling in. And where I was working in that furrier's department was rather close to that place. So through the window, I could see what is happening there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I could see people who were being brought up there, they were walked up on the ramp to the pit, and some of them I recognized. Some very noble, religious rabbis, who were shot like dogs. And if God could allow that, then the question is, is there a God? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wish you wouldn't have asked that question. Why was it important for you to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess it is only important that some other people will realize that such a thing has happened, and know the truth, and hopefully will not happen again. It is not really important to me. Won't do anything for me. My children, unfortunately, don't seem to be very interested in it either.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remarried.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1968, a little more than two years after my wife passed away. I was with a seven-year-old baby, was born in 1949-- '59, excuse me. And a 15-year-old, 16-year-old baby, whatever, was born 1949. I mean, a girl. And I hired different housekeepers to keep things going. And things didn't work out. There were more arguments then-- than a family life, and I kept changing housekeepers. Every month I had somebody else. Couldn't-- Couldn't make sense out of it. At any rate, I met Doris, my second wife, at the place where we worked together. I have made her my assistant eventually, because I was growing very rapidly in the company. When I became vice president of operations, she-- she became the manager of the quality control and production control. And she did very well. She's very talented.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doris has now a one-man show in the community theater. She has 43 paintings there, with her name being right on the marquee in light, Doris Lipschutz. You know where that theater is? You don't. In [INAUDIBLE]? See, this is why the walls are empty, because of the pictures are gone. And we-- she had two children. She was divorced.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw a life and a-- a future for us as being in both of all our interests. This was not a marriage we fell in love. I mean, this is-- was probably, I would say, a very practical marriage on both sides. But we're doing very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And together, you have how many children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't have any children together.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean blended, not biologically.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, blended, yeah. Well, we have four children and nine grandchildren. The grandchildren are from age 11, approximately, to 23. Our oldest granddaughter graduated college last summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your two daughters have children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My two daughters. My older daughter has a son. And a daughter and a son. The daughter who I was mention graduated. The son is now attending college. And the younger daughter had two daughters. One is 15. One is 12. These two girls are geniuses. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Beyond-- beyond belief. They must have about 150 IQ.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they show an interest in your past?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only-- well, there was a very, very interesting thing. Mark, who is the only boy, or son of my youngest-- my oldest daughter, who is now in college. In-- his senior year of high school, he wrote an essay on the subject of Holocaust. And it was related to the movie. And if you remember, in the movie they say how many people are alive today because of what Schindler has [INAUDIBLE]. And he says, this is the first time it dawned on me that I am one of the 6,000 who would not have been here if my-- my grandfather wasn't a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about a cousin that you were reunited with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. We lived in Poland, in Krakow. We had-- the whole time, we  lived in Krakow. One day when I was probably three or four years old, or five years maybe, because I still remember it. One of my cousins, my old-- oldest aunt's daughter, was getting married. And right after her getting married in Krakow-- she-- her husband was from Lithuania. They moved to Lithuania. I remember now, going with my parents to the railroad station, and they were traveling to Lithuania. Eventually became very wealthy in Lithuania. They had many factories, and [INAUDIBLE]. When the Russians occupied Lithuania during-- in 1939, they managed to escape from Lithuania to England, because their son at that time was studying in England, though he lived in Lithuania. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To make the story short, after-- when-- after the war when the-- when I knew that they managed to get out of Lithuania into-- into England, I lived in Bergen-Belsen, and there were-- there was an English zone of occupation of Germany. I walked up to a-- a-- like a jeep-- jeep-like car, where there were two ladies sitting, and they were English relief ladies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in my-- in my German, because they spoke German-- I couldn't speak English at the time-- I told them a story. I tell them a story, I had a cousin who lived in Lithuania. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I know that she went to England when the war started, can you find her for me? What is her name? Fania Feinberg. Fania Feinberg, she says, I know her. She's in charge of the whole operation. [CRYING] So you made contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Made contact, and eventually, she-- he died, her husband died. We came to United States, she visited-- visited us in the United States. She went back to England. She died. And she had one son. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The son eventually moved to Israel. [INAUDIBLE] I don't really know the details. He met a lady from the United States who worked for United Airlines. And they got married. They moved to Minneapolis. She's retired from United Airlines. And last year, they visited the Holocaust Museum in Washington, where I am registered. And he-- he said, I have to find out-- he told me that story. I have to find out whether any of the Lipschutzes alive. And he says it was so many different Lipschutzes. He says, but yeah, but I'm only interested in Lipschutz from Krakow. So he had already narrowed it down. And then somehow, he knew something that I did not know. That I am named after my father's father, Joseph. And he said, Joseph, probably him. And they made him-- so he wanted to get in contact with me. He says, they said, he can't-- we won't let you get in contact with him, you can only get in contact with him through us. You-- you-- they gave him a sheet of paper, a special thing he can fill out whatever he want, and they would mail it to me. And they did. And he says, I am such and such, and this is my telephone number and address whatever, and we got in contact. And a couple months, they-- they both came to visit us here. As a matter of fact, right now, they-- they're in Hawaii. I got a card yesterday. Yeah, Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we show your pictures, is there anything else that you haven't talked about, that you haven't told me, that you would like to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably so, but I don't remember. Probably so. Probably after you-- you will leave, I will have many of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why don't we stop now, and we'll show your pictures and your documents. Joe, we're now going to share some of your pictures. Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, when he was very young. Before he even got married. The photograph was made 1907, he was married in 1916. This photograph was recovered by some miraculous way. A friend, who lived, at that time, in France, was given this photograph by my father with an inscription on the back. And after the war, we had no photographs of anybody at all. But that friend somehow found out that my father's son, which means me, survived, and decided I should have the picture, not him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Abraham Lipschutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Rozalia. This picture was recovered from her identification card, which was-- which was issued to her in ghetto, in 1941, I guess. And reproduced in this larger size. This is the only one I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her full name was Rozalia Lipschutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosalia [? Lieberman. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a composite picture. It isn't really a photograph of the people, it's a photograph of photographs. The picture of my father was copied from the portrait that we just looked at a minute ago. And the person who made the portrait out of that photograph made my father look a little bit older. And the person who made-- the same person making the picture of my mother make her look a little bit younger, so as to make-- make them look as a compatible couple. And they sure are. This is beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Lipschutz and Rozalia Lipschutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: They both lived in Krakow. My mother was born in [INAUDIBLE], and my father was born in Krakow. [INAUDIBLE] This is my pride. Move Move it down. OK. This is a photograph of me, Joseph Lipschutz. This photograph was made in 1938, at the time I was 20 years old. This is the only picture from before the war that we have actually managed to salvage. And I do not remember how. But I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a copy of the Schindler's list. My name appears under the number 69,187. This list was made actually-- this is not the original list that Schindler made in Plaszow in order to assemble his people to go to Czechoslovakia. This list was make, I think, in April 1945. While the Schindler's camp was in [INAUDIBLE] and operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And point to your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is with [INAUDIBLE] [? fingers ?]. Oh, I shouldn't be touching this, I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] The-- the top of the list shows the relationship between the Schindler's camp and the Gross-Rosen concentration camp, because a very large, major camp. The top line here says, KL concentration lager, Gross-Rosen, Brunnlitz Liste. Or meaning, the list which was of people who went to-- to Brunnlitz. This is a ketubah, Jewish marriage license of my first wife and myself, dating back to 1948 in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on. Joe, can you lean back for me just a bit? Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually not the marriage license, marriage certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can say that now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is actually not a marriage license, but it is a marriage certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Hannah, a photograph. Appearing on a document issued just prior to our immigration to the United States. Testifying that she's healthy, and therefore allowed to go to-- to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold-- hold it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is similar document, actually the same kind of document as I described before for Hannah, except with my picture. Also testifying that I am healthy enough to be immigrating to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Joseph Lipschutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year this was done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the last date is November the 8th, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Ah, get a glare.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a snapshot of my wife, my late wife Hannah, and myself, taken most likely sometimes in the middle '50s, after we arrived in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure that I know where we were living at the time, but probably in [INAUDIBLE], and it was I think, number 5 [? Patent ?] Street. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is our family prior to my wife's death. My wife Hannah, my own oldest daughter Rosalie, my younger daughter Lynn, and myself. It was taken probably in the early '60s. Looks like my younger, Lynn, is about three years old, and she was born 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would be most likely Philadelphia, or one of our trips, which I couldn't tell. This is my wife Doris, and myself, Joe. Who-- we got married in July 11, 1968. We're doing quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a combined family of Doris's and myself-- and mine. With her children, herself, her mother, a couple of her aunts, but mostly-- oh, and I'm also there. I didn't realize that. Well, this is a similar picture at Doris's parents' house on-- at-- at a picnic. And it has both of my daughters, Rosalie and Lynn, all four grandchildren, myself, Doris, and her children.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson's, Mark, bar mitzvah. It is his mother, my oldest daughter Rosalie, her husband, Harvey, and her-- his-- his sister Helene. And he himself, Mark Andrew. This picture depicts my wife, Doris, the four-- four grandchildren Helene, Mark, Jessica, and Ashley, and myself. I do not remember the occasion when this picture was taken. But it is rather recent. This is a picture of my newly found cousin, who after 50 years, contacted me through Holocaust Museum in Washington. This my cousin, Steve, his wife [? Lenore, ?] and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this your sole surviving cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sole surviving cousin that is alive. There were others who since passed away. And there's supposedly a couple other distant cousins who live in strange countries, like Sweden and other places. And I am not in contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a marriage certificate of my parents. I have received this in 1988 through my former brother-in-law who went to-- who visited Poland. And it shows my father here, and the date of his birth date, et cetera. My mother's. And the fact that they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a birth certificate of my older brother, Samuel. Again, I received sometime in 1988 a copy of-- this a copy of the original entry in the book in Krakow. This, in turn, is a birth certificate of my sister, Sabina. Obtained the same way.
